query: management guidelines for small, calcified lung nodules < 3 cm with regular borders

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional approach is best for managing an SPN. Assessing nodule size, appearance, patient risk factors, and patient preference is integral in treating SPNs. The following is a summary of the 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines :

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with ground-glass nodules <5 mm should have a repeat CT scan in 2 to 3 years, while for those with nodules >5 mm, a repeat CT scan in 3 months with subsequent CT scans for up to 3 years if the nodule persists is recommended. A biopsy or surgical excision is recommended if the nodule persists for 3 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: No more testing is required if a nodule has been stable for 2 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Imaging -- Computed Tomography
retrieved_docs.content: A nodule can have characteristics that favor it to be benign or malignant. Size is an important consideration when stratifying nodules, as is the density of the nodule i.e., solid or ground glass. Irregular non-spherical nodules with spiculated margins are more suspicious of malignancy than smooth nodules. Pleural based nodules are usually benign. [8] Patterns of calcification such as central, diffuse, popcorn type, or lamellated favor a benign nodule. [9] Peripheral lung masses with pleural and extrapleural extension can be associated with rib erosions. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Follow-up recommendations according to the Lungs-RADs classification are as follows: Nodules classified as a Lung-RADS Category 0 must be compared to the previous complete lung imaging before being classified. Patients with category 1 and 2 findings may continue with yearly low-dose CT scan. Those with category 3 findings should be followed up with a 6-month low-dose CT scan. Individuals with category 4A findings should be followed up with a low-dose CT scan in 3 months or have a PET/CT scan if there is a >8 mm solid component. Patients with category 4B and 4X should get a full-dose chest CT scan with or without contrast, PET/CT, biopsy, or repeat low-dose CT scan at 1 month.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the diagnostic criteria for a solitary pulmonary nodule. Differentiate the various ways in which a patient may present with a solitary pulmonary nodule. Implement evidence-based guidelines for the management and follow-up of a patient with solitary pulmonary nodule. Assess interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the management of patients with a solitary pulmonary nodule and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with part-solid nodules should have repeat CT imaging at 3 months. If, at 3 months, the solid component is <5 mm, then annual CT imaging is recommended for at least 3 years. A biopsy or surgical excision is warranted if it is >5 mm at 3 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Solid nodules between 8 mm and 30 mm are divided into low, moderate, and high pretest probability groups. Patients with a low pretest probability should have low-dose CT scans at 3 to 6 months, 9 to 12 months, and 18 to 24 months. Those with a moderate pretest probability should have PET or serial CT scans. Individuals with a high pretest probability should go straight to surgical diagnosis with either biopsy or surgical excision.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Those with non-solid nodules <5 mm need no further evaluation. Those  with >5 mm nodules should get annual CT scans for 3 years. If the non-solid nodule is >1 cm, repeat imaging at 3 months, biopsy, or surgical excision is warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In 2013, the American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP) developed its guidelines for SPNs and states that CT imaging should be utilized if a nodule is found on a CXR. These findings must be compared to old images to evaluate any change.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Biopsy Techniques and Clinical Significance -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: New lesions on chest imaging should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team involving chest specialists, surgeons, and radiologists to decide the next best management. [3] A lung biopsy is required when there is a need to provide a specific diagnosis. Indications for PTLB according to the BTS guidelines (in near-verbatim language) are as follows: [3] New or enlarging mass or solitary nodule on chest radiography that is not amenable to diagnosis by CT or bronchoscopy shows it is unlikely to be accessible by bronchoscopy. Multiple nodules in a patient with no previous history of malignancy, who have had a prolonged remission or more than one primary malignancy. Persistent focal infiltrates for which no diagnosis has been made by sputum, or bronchoscopy, blood culture, serology. Hilar mass.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ground-glass nodules: <6 mm require no routine follow-up. If >6 mm, CT should be repeated every 6 to 12 months, then every 2 years for a total of 5 years. If parts of the nodule are solid, consider resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Partly solid nodules: <6 mm require no routine follow-up. If >6 mm, repeat CT at 3 to 6 months; if the nodule continues to grow or has a persistent solid component >6 mm, the patient is deemed high risk, and resection should be considered. A CT should be performed annually for 5 years if the nodule is unchanged from prior imaging and the solid component is <6 mm.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Lung Lesion Biopsy -- Contraindications -- General Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: These factors are considered contraindications because they increase the risk of the following: Failure to obtain an adequate specimen is unlikely Severely injuring the patient in an environment where appropriate treatment cannot be quickly provided Inability to extend the patient's survival or improve quality of life (Refer to " Bleeding Risks " and " Noncooperative, Nontrained, or Over-Sedated Patients" for more detailed information on these various risks)

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If multiple nodules are present, the patient is recommended to have a CT scan at 3 to 6 months. [5] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Solitary Pulmonary Nodule -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Solid nodules <8 mm are more likely to be benign and are divided into 2 groups: those with or without risk for malignancy. If there is no risk for malignancy and the nodule is <4 mm, follow-up is optional. For nodules 4 mm to 6 mm, follow-up CT is performed at 12 months. For nodules 6 mm to 8 mm, follow-up is done at 6 to 12 months and then again at 18 to 24 months. Patients with risk factors for malignancy and nodules <4 mm should have repeat CT at 12 months. If the nodule is 4 mm to 6 mm and the patient has risk factors for malignancy, a follow-up CT scan at 6 to 12 months and again at 18 to 24 months is recommended. For patients with risk factors for malignancy and 6 mm to 8 mm nodules, follow-up with a repeat CT scan at 3 to 6 months, 9 to 12 months, and again at 24 months is suggested.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic computed tomography protocol for the thorax that begins without contrast material and then proceeds with contrast and additional sections represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan. A chest CT scan serves as the primary imaging method for detecting a variety of mediastinal abnormalities. Findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum, a mass of the posterior mediastinum, a mass of the superior mediastinum, a generic mediastinal mass, and a mediastinal mass containing calcium are all classified as “كتلة منصفية” (mediastinal mass) and are identified through the chest CT scanning technique. In addition to mediastinal lesions, a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is another pathology that can be visualized using the chest CT scan. Thus, the specialized thoracic CT protocol is a narrower subset of chest CT imaging, which is employed to characterize mediastinal masses—including those with calcification—and diffuse pulmonary infiltrates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of posterior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of superior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass, and computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass containing calcium are all classified as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). These CT‑detected lesions, whether located in the mid, posterior, or superior mediastinum or showing calcification, belong to the broader category of mediastinal masses, a distinction that guides further diagnostic evaluation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections represents a more specific, narrower form of a ct scan chest. The ct scan chest is the imaging modality employed as the finding method for both computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass and computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass containing calcium. In clinical practice, chest CT imaging is used to detect mediastinal abnormalities, and when calcium is present within a mediastinal mass, the same ct scan chest technique provides the necessary diagnostic information. The detailed thoracic CT protocol that begins without contrast and then adds contrast‑enhanced sections exemplifies a refined approach within the broader category of chest CT scans for evaluating mediastinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2337
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of superior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass, and computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass containing calcium are all identified as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass). These CT‑detected lesions, whether located in the mid or superior mediastinum or showing calcification, represent variations of the same pathological entity—a mediastinal mass—highlighting the importance of recognizing their common classification for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 465
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest (CT scan chest) encompasses several specific imaging protocols, including diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, diagnostic thoracic CT followed by contrast administration and additional sections, and contrast‑enhanced axial tomography of the thorax; each of these protocols is a narrower, more detailed variant of the general chest CT examination. All of these CT techniques serve as the primary method for detecting mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية). Through CT imaging, clinicians can identify mediastinal masses located in distinct compartments—mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, and superior‑mediastinal regions—and can also recognize masses that contain calcium. Thus, chest CT, in its various contrast‑based and non‑contrast forms, is the essential diagnostic tool for characterizing the presence, location, and composition of mediastinal masses, informing subsequent clinical management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum, a central subdivision of the thorax, serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower respiratory diseases such as lung and mediastinal abscesses (خراج) and is implicated in mediastinal disorders (اضطراب منصفي) including effusions. Mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية)—encompassing mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, and calcified variants—along with mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (unilateral right, unilateral left, and bilateral) are key thoracic pathologies. Chest computed tomography (CT), classified under imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract, is the primary diagnostic modality for visualizing these conditions. Standard chest CT, thoracic CT without contrast, and specialized thoracic CT scans (e.g., “thoracic CT scan: mediastinal‑pleural fistula”) function as parent procedures that generate specific findings such as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” and “mediastinal effusion.” The chest CT hierarchy also includes pulmonary angiographic imaging as a child study. Closed chest drainage is an interventional procedure linked to the thoracic subdivision and guided by chest CT imaging. Breast CT, while primarily a breast imaging study, is positioned inversely to radiographic breast imaging and is subsumed under the broader chest CT category, reflecting overlap between thoracic and breast imaging. Overall, the network emphasizes the mediastinum as a pivotal structure in lower respiratory disease, abscess formation, mass lesions, and fistulous complications, with chest CT—particularly non‑contrast thoracic protocols—serving as the essential tool for diagnosis and procedural planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1327
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material represents a specific, narrower form of a chest CT scan. This imaging modality, referred to as a ct scan chest, serves as the primary method for detecting mediastinal abnormalities. In particular, a ct scan chest is used to identify masses located in the mid mediastinum as well as masses situated in the superior mediastinum, with each of these findings being directly linked to the chest CT imaging technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: CT in identifying a wide range of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities. Collectively, these relationships delineate an integrated framework in which mediastinal pathology—ranging from abscesses, segmental disorders, and various mediastinal masses (including calcium‑bearing lesions) to pleural‑mediastinal fistulas and miliary infiltrates—is situated within the category of other lower respiratory disease and is primarily evaluated through chest CT imaging. The hierarchical structure links general CT scanning to specialized chest CT procedures, to specific diagnostic findings, while positioning conventional chest x‑ray as a related but subordinate imaging option, thereby supporting comprehensive assessment and management of mediastinal and lower‑respiratory conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1024
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the thorax performed as a diagnostic study without contrast material and computerized axial tomography of the thorax with contrast are both specific, narrower forms of a chest CT scan. The chest CT scan serves as the imaging method used to detect a variety of thoracic findings. Computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of posterior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of superior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass, and computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass containing calcium are each identified on chest CT as findings and are each classified as “كتلة منصفية” (mediastinal mass). In addition, computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate is another finding that can be identified by chest CT, representing a diffuse, tiny‑nodular pattern of pulmonary involvement. Together, these relationships illustrate that detailed CT techniques (with or without contrast) are subsets of the broader chest CT modality, and that chest CT is the diagnostic tool used to reveal both mediastinal masses—including those located in the mid, posterior, or superior mediastinum and those containing calcium—and miliary pulmonary infiltrates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2133
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest can reveal several findings that are classified as mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية). Specifically, a mass located in the superior mediastinum identified on chest CT, a general mediastinal mass seen on chest CT, and a mediastinal mass that contains calcium on chest CT are all considered types of mediastinal mass. Recognizing these imaging appearances as mediastinal masses is essential for appropriate diagnostic evaluation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest can reveal several findings that are classified as mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية). Specifically, a mass located in the posterior mediastinum identified on chest CT, a general mediastinal mass seen on chest CT, and a mediastinal mass that contains calcium on chest CT are all considered examples of mediastinal masses. Recognizing these imaging appearances as mediastinal masses is essential for further diagnostic evaluation and appropriate patient management.
